Headline: Social Networks
Text: From dial-up bulletin boards to the platform giants — and to the states that learned to switch them off. Where social networks rose, were bought, and were banned. A story by MICRI group 1.

Headline: Before Social Media: The Rise of Bulletin Board Systems
Text: In the late 1970s, Bulletin Board Systems (BBS) emerged as rudimentary platforms that users could access via modem to exchange messages, share files, and participate in online discussions, forming some of the earliest digital communities. A key development came in 1978 with the Computer Bulletin Board System (CBBS), built in Chicago by hobbyists Ward Christensen and Randy Suess, allowing messages to be stored and shared between users, an early form of a social network. It declined with the rise of the Internet in the 1990s.

Headline: SixDegrees: The World's First True Social Network
Text:
SixDegrees.com was launched in New York and is widely recognized as the first true social networking site in history. Founded by Andrew Weinreich, the platform brought the "six degrees of separation" theory to the web, allowing users for the first time to create a real profile, add friends, and browse their connections.
SixDegrees was revolutionary because it introduced many of the features that remain central to social media today: real user profiles, friends lists, and the ability to explore the networks of other users.
Despite its initial success and approximately 3.5 million registered users, the platform shut down between 2000 and 2001. SixDegrees was simply ahead of its time. In the late 1990s, internet connections were slow, relatively few people were online on a regular basis, and the public was not yet ready to build and maintain relationships in a digital environment. Nevertheless, it paved the way for the development of modern social media.

Headline: How LinkedIn Reinvented Professional Networking
Text: Founded in California in 2003 by Reid Hoffman and a team from Silicon Valley, LinkedIn set out to transform traditional professional relationships into a dynamic digital network. Starting as a minimalist platform focused strictly on profiles and connections, it quickly evolved into an essential global infrastructure for networking and job searching, enabling professionals to showcase their careers and allowing companies to actively source talent.

Headline: Facebook: From a Harvard Dorm Room to a Global Empire
Text: TheFacebook is launched by Mark Zuckerberg and other Harvard students. Initially reserved for the university's students, it expands rapidly to become the most widely used social network in the world.

Headline: The Three Friends Who Changed the Internet Forever
Text: YouTube is born as a project by three former PayPal employees: Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim. After abandoning the initial idea of a dating site, it becomes the world's leading platform for video sharing.
Media Caption: The first YouTube video, titled "Me at the zoo". It was uploaded on April 23, 2005, by YouTube co-founder Jawed Karim

Headline: Twitter and the Microblogging Revolution
Text: Jack Dorsey publishes the first tweet in history. Twitter introduces microblogging, hashtags, and retweets, becoming one of the main tools for the spread of news in real time.
Media Caption: The first tweet in history, posted by Twitter co-founder Jack Dorsey on March 21, 2006

Headline: Social Networks as Drivers of Revolution
Text:
Facebook and Twitter take on a central role during the Arab Spring, facilitating the organization, communication, and spread of the protests.Citizens in countries where freedom of expression had long been suppressed found new channels to connect with one another and challenge the existing power structures.
Western media described the Arab Spring as the "Internet revolutions." The movements became so widespread and influential that, in 2011, four heads of state were forced to resign or were killed: Zine El Abidine Ben Ali in Tunisia, Hosni Mubarak in Egypt, Muammar Gaddafi in Libya, and Ali Abdullah Saleh in Yemen.
Source: Wikipedia — Social media's role in the Arab Spring

Headline: Goodbye WhatsApp in China: The Great Firewall Grows Stronger
Text:
In September 2017, WhatsApp effectively became unusable in China. There was no official announcement of a ban—rather, a gradual shutdown left the service unreachable for most users within days, part of Beijing's broader tightening of internet control.
The block wasn't sudden: users had already reported growing issues, from difficulty sending images and videos to chat slowdowns, culminating in total inaccessibility, a pattern typical of China's censorship and filtering system.
The key factor behind the decision was WhatsApp's end-to-end encryption, which protects conversations even from network operators, a hard feature to reconcile with China's cyberspace rules.
The move fits a wider strategy: China has already blocked Western platforms favoring domestic alternatives like WeChat that are easier for authorities to monitor and that now dominate daily digital life in the country.

Headline: The Deal That Created TikTok: ByteDance Buys Musical.ly
Text:
Almost four years after Musical.ly's inception, the Chinese tech giant ByteDance decided to acquire the platform. The offer was impossible to refuse: around 750 million euros, and Musical.ly gave in to the merger under the TikTok brand. The platform began to spread rapidly: today it can be found in over 170 countries and has more than 1.5 billion users worldwide. The platform's strengths are two: the format of short videos accompanied by music, filters, and special effects, and its content monetization system.
With users spending an average of 34 hours a month on TikTok, its rapid hold on younger audiences and creators has shaken things up. As a result, the web giants were forced to imitate it: Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts were conceived along the same lines as TikTok.

Headline: The Year the Digital World Changed Forever
Text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, social networks experienced a historic surge in usage. They became essential tools for communication, studying, working, and staying informed, but the problem of misinformation also grew.
Media Caption: SARS-CoV-2 the virus that causes COVID-19, isolated from a patient.

Headline: Myanmar: When Facebook Went Dark Under Military Rule
Text: Following the February 2021 coup in Myanmar, the military junta blocked Facebook—officially citing stability and disinformation concerns—in a strategic move to suppress civil resistance, hinder the coordination of nationwide protests, and tighten state control over the country's primary information flow.
Media Caption: Myanmar’s struggle becomes a global call for freedom

Headline: From Facebook to Meta: Reinventing a Tech Giant
Text: In October 2021, Facebook Inc. rebranded as Meta, shifting its corporate identity to signal a long-term strategy centered on building the "metaverse"—a 3D, immersive digital ecosystem transcending traditional social networks. While Mark Zuckerberg framed this move as a technological leap "beyond" current boundaries, the announcement sparked intense debate; critics viewed the dramatic rebranding as a strategic distraction to deflect global political pressure following the explosive revelations of whistleblower Frances Haugen regarding the platform's algorithms, disinformation, and public safety.

Headline: Elon Musk Takes Over Twitter
Text:
With a lightning-fast tweet reading “The bird is freed,” Elon Musk formalized the purchase of Twitter for $44 billion.
While the American and European far right rejoiced at the change in course and free speech advocates celebrated the event, critics immediately raised concerns: according to many analysts, loosening the reins of control risked opening the floodgates to an uncontrollable wave of political disinformation and fake news.

Headline: The Day Brazil Turned X Off
Text: For 39 days in 2024, 212 million Brazilians lost access to X. Not a glitch — a court order. It started after the January 8, 2023 Brasília riots. Brazil's Supreme Court had blocked accounts tied to the attack over disinformation concerns. In April 2024, Musk said he'd reinstate them anyway, calling it a free speech stand. Justice Alexandre de Moraes called it an attack on Brazilian sovereignty. X shut its Brazil office rather than name a legal representative, as required by law. On August 30, de Moraes ordered a nationwide block, upheld unanimously days later by the full Court. It also froze Starlink's assets — also Musk's — to secure fine payments, and fined Brazilians dodging the ban via VPN. X blinked first: $5.2 million in fines paid, a legal rep named, flagged accounts pulled. The ban lifted on October 8, 2024. It's likely the first time a state fully switched off a major global platform — and won. Musk's "absolute free speech" line met a market he couldn't afford to lose.
Media Caption: São Paulo streets: the X ban becomes a rallying cry against judicial censorship

Headline: Sovereign Darkness: Iran’s Internet Blackout
Text: As protests against the regime continue to grow. The nationwide blackout was imposed after demonstrations erupted in early January 2026 over soaring inflation, economic hardship and growing public dissatisfaction with the government. What began as economic protests quickly turned into broader calls for political change. The internet shutdown has left millions unable to communicate, access independent news or share images of the unrest. Human rights groups say the measure is intended to limit the flow of information and conceal the security forces’ response to the demonstrations.
Media Caption: Popular protests in Nishapur, Iran, on 19 January, 2026
